planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting, or purchase bedding plants; in zone 9, plant in spring through early fall in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; pentas is one of the most reliable butterfly and hummingbird plants for hot, humid Gulf Coast summers.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; deadhead spent clusters to encourage continuous bloom. Pentas lanceolata (star flower or pentas) is a tropical East African shrub grown as a warm-season annual or perennial in zones 9–12, producing dense, 3-to-4-inch, domed clusters of small, star-shaped, 5-petaled flowers in red, pink, lavender, white, and bicolor combinations on bushy, upright plants that bloom continuously from spring through frost and beyond in frost-free climates. It is among the best and most reliable butterfly plants for Gulf Coast gardens — the flat-topped flower clusters are perfectly designed for butterfly feeding, attracting Gulf Fritillaries, Sulphurs, Swallowtails, Monarchs, and many other species in numbers not equaled by most other plants. Pentas also attracts hummingbirds to red and pink cultivars. In zone 9, it thrives through the hot, humid summer when many other plants struggle, blooming non-stop in summer heat; the plant often survives mild winters in zone 9b as a perennial, producing larger plants and more blooms in subsequent seasons. The 'Graffiti' and 'Lucky Star' series are among the most compact and floriferous.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pentas
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Butterfly Plants
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Summer Annuals

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost, or propagate by stem cuttings; plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; cigar flower is an outstanding hummingbird plant for Gulf Coast gardens and produces non-stop blooms through the hot, humid summer.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; trim lightly after main bloom flushes to maintain compact habit. Cuphea ignea (cigar flower) is a Mexican native tender perennial grown as a warm-season annual in most climates, producing an endless succession of tubular, bright orange-red flowers 1 inch long with a distinctive black and white tip that gives each flower the appearance of a tiny lit cigar — the most descriptive common name in horticulture. The flowers bloom non-stop from late spring through frost on bushy, compact plants with dark-green, lance-shaped leaves; hummingbirds visit the tubular flowers avidly, making cigar plant one of the best hummingbird plants for hot-summer gardens. In zone 9–10, it frequently survives mild winters and grows as a perennial, becoming substantially larger and more prolific than annual specimens. The related Cuphea hyssopifolia (Mexican heather, dwarf cigar plant) has much smaller, lilac-purple flowers and more compact habit. Cigar plant's heat tolerance, disease resistance, and near-constant bloom make it outstanding for massed planting in zone 9 gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flowers

planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; coral plant is a fast-growing, drought-tolerant tropical perennial that produces its best growth and blooms in warm, sunny conditions, yet also tolerates more shade than most flowering shrubs.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; coral plant is drought-tolerant once established and actually blooms more heavily under mild drought stress. Russelia equisetiformis (coral plant or firecracker fern) is a Mexican native producing a dense, weeping, fountain-like mound of slender, rush-like, bright-green to gray-green photosynthetic stems (reduced leaves — hence the species name 'equisetiformis,' meaning 'horsetail-like') hung with hundreds of slender, 1-inch, bright-red tubular flowers from spring through frost. The effect is of an almost waterfall-like cascade of green stems and red flowers — one of the most graceful and romantic ornamental shrubs for tropical and subtropical gardens. Ruby-throated hummingbirds and butterflies visit the tubular flowers extensively. Coral plant is exceptionally heat, drought, and salt-tolerant once established, performing well in coastal gardens, under dry overhangs, and in containers on sunny patios. In zone 9, it dies back in hard freezes but resprouts reliably from roots; in zone 10–12, it is an evergreen perennial. It roots quickly from stem cuttings and can be propagated freely for mass plantings.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Shrubs
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ornamental Shrubs

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost, or purchase transplants; in zone 9, plant in spring for summer and fall bloom in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; melampodium is one of the most heat-tolerant and carefree yellow-flowering annuals for Gulf Coast gardens.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting or direct sow in warm soil; plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; no deadheading needed. Melampodium paludosum (butter daisy or melampodium) is a Central American native annual producing cheerful, 1.5-inch, bright-yellow daisy flowers with a yellow center continuously from late spring through hard frost on neat, mounded, self-cleaning plants that require no deadheading — spent flowers drop cleanly. This combination of bright, warm color, non-stop bloom, compact mounding habit, heat tolerance, and carefree nature makes melampodium one of the top summer annuals for hot, humid Gulf Coast gardens, where it outperforms many other yellow-flowering annuals. It is outstanding as an edging plant, in masses for summer color beds, and in containers. Unlike many yellow daisies, it is resistant to spider mites and maintains clean, disease-free foliage through the summer heat. The 'Medallion' and 'Derby' series are the most widely planted; 'Lemon Delight' has lighter, lemon-yellow flowers.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flowers
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Annual Flowers

common_name: Turnera (White Buttercup, Sulphur Alder, Politician's Bush)

planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; Turnera subulata thrives in the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast summers and blooms continuously from spring through frost in zone 9.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; once established, turnera is drought-tolerant and thrives with minimal care. Turnera subulata (white buttercup or turnera) is a warm-climate shrubby perennial from South America producing masses of cheerful, 5-petaled, white flowers each about 1 inch across, with a distinctive yellow eye at the center — each flower opens for only a single day (a brief daily flowering phenomenon characteristic of the genus), but the continuous succession of new buds from spring through frost maintains constant bloom. The yellow-centered white flowers bloom prolifically on compact, rounded bushes with toothed, dark-green, slightly aromatic leaves. Turnera is outstanding for Gulf Coast gardens where it grows as a near-evergreen perennial shrub in zone 9–10, providing reliable bloom through summer heat and humidity when many other plants struggle; in colder zones, it is used as a summer annual. It naturalizes in warm climates and can become mildly weedy in tropical gardens. Highly tolerant of drought, poor soil, and coastal salt spray once established.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Plants
